topics.topic_name: Understanding the Mean as a Measure of Central Tendency

content.text: The mean (or arithmetic average) is a measure of central tendency that represents the 'centre' of a dataset. Unlike simply being the midpoint between extremes, the mean has a special balancing property: the sum of distances from the mean to values on the left equals the sum of distances to values on the right.
For two numbers, the mean is exactly halfway between them. For example, the mean of 3 and 7 is (3 + 7) / 2 = 5, which lies exactly in the middle of these two values on a number line.

content.title: Mean as Balance Point
content.text: The mean acts like a fulcrum or balance point in data. If we place weights at each data value on a number line, the mean is the exact point where the distribution balances. The total distance of values below the mean equals the total distance of values above the mean.

This balancing property holds true for datasets of any size. When we have multiple data points, the sum of all distances to the left of the mean equals the sum of all distances to the right of the mean, creating a perfect balance.

content.title: Example: Balancing Property with Three Values
content.content: Consider data: 1, 5, 8. The mean = (1 + 5 + 8) / 3 = 14 / 3 ≈ 4.67. Left side distances: |1 - 4.67| = 3.67. Right side distances: |5 - 4.67| + |8 - 4.67| = 0.33 + 3.33 = 3.67. The distances balance perfectly, confirming the mean is the balance point.

topics.topic_name: The Mean is Unique as a Centre

content.text: An important property of the mean is its uniqueness. There is exactly one value for any dataset that acts as a perfect balance point. If we choose any value smaller than the mean, the distances on the right side will be greater than the left side. If we choose any value larger than the mean, the distances on the left will be greater than the right side.
This uniqueness makes the mean particularly useful in statistics. Unlike other measures, there can never be two or more values that serve as equally valid balance points for a given dataset.

content.title: Why the Mean is Unique
content.text: For any dataset, only one value can satisfy the condition where the sum of distances to values less than it equals the sum of distances to values greater than it. This makes the mean a unique and powerful measure of central tendency.

topics.topic_name: Effect of Adding Values Greater Than the Mean

content.text: When a new value greater than the mean is added to a dataset, the overall mean increases. This happens because the new value pulls the balance point to the right. The larger the new value, the greater the increase in the mean.
For example, if a dataset has mean 11 and we add a value of 20, the new mean will increase to accommodate the larger value while maintaining the balance property.

content.title: Adding Values and Mean Change
content.text: When adding a value > mean: the new mean increases. When adding a value < mean: the new mean decreases. When adding a value = mean: the mean stays the same. This follows from the principle of balance and weighted averages.

content.title: Example: Adding a Value Greater Than Mean
content.content: Original dataset: 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 with mean = 8. Add value 20. New mean = (4 + 6 + 8 + 10 + 12 + 20) / 6 = 60 / 6 = 10. The mean increased from 8 to 10 because 20 > 8.

topics.topic_name: Adding Multiple Values Without Changing the Mean

content.text: It is possible to add multiple values to a dataset and keep the mean unchanged. This occurs when the values we add balance each other out—values above the mean and values below the mean can cancel each other's effect on the overall balance.

For instance, if the current mean is 10, adding one value of 8 (which is 2 below mean) and one value of 12 (which is 2 above mean) will keep the mean unchanged because their deviations balance perfectly.

content.title: Balancing New Values
content.text: To add values without changing the mean, ensure that the sum of deviations (distances from the mean) of values below the mean equals the sum of deviations of values above the mean. This maintains the balance property.

content.title: Example: Adding Two Values to Keep Mean Constant
content.content: Original dataset: 6, 8, 10, 12 with mean = 9. Add values 7 and 11. New mean = (6 + 8 + 10 + 12 + 7 + 11) / 6 = 54 / 6 = 9. The mean remains 9 because (7 - 9) + (11 - 9) = -2 + 2 = 0, so the deviations balance.

topics.topic_name: Adding a Constant to All Values

content.text: When we add the same constant value to every element in a dataset, the mean also increases by that same constant. If the original mean is 'a' and we add constant 'c' to each value, the new mean becomes 'a + c'.

content.title: Effect of Adding Constants
content.text: If original mean = a, and we add constant k to all values, then new mean = a + k. This is because the sum increases by (n × k) where n is the number of values, and dividing by n gives us the constant k added to the original mean.
For example, if data {8, 3, 10, 13, 4, 6, 7, 7, 8, 8, 5} has mean 7.18, and we add 10 to each value, the new data becomes {18, 13, 20, 23, 14, 16, 17, 17, 18, 18, 15} with new mean 17.18.

content.title: Algebraic Proof: Adding Constant c
content.content: Original mean: a = (X₁ + X₂ + ... + Xₙ) / n. New mean with +c: = [(X₁ + c) + (X₂ + c) + ... + (Xₙ + c)] / n = [(X₁ + X₂ + ... + Xₙ) + nc] / n = (X₁ + X₂ + ... + Xₙ)/n + nc/n = a + c. Therefore, new mean = a + c.

topics.topic_name: Subtracting a Constant from All Values

content.text: Similarly, when we subtract the same constant from every value in a dataset, the mean decreases by that constant. If the original mean is 'a' and we subtract constant 'c', the new mean becomes 'a - c'.
This property extends the principle of data transformation. Whether we are adding or subtracting, the mean shifts by exactly the same amount.

content.title: Effect of Subtracting Constants
content.text: If original mean = a, and we subtract constant k from all values, then new mean = a - k. The relative positions of data points stay the same; only the scale reference point shifts.

content.title: Example: Subtracting a Constant
content.content: If data has mean 100 and we subtract 5 from each value, the new mean = 100 - 5 = 95. The spread and distribution of data relative to the mean remain unchanged.

topics.topic_name: Multiplying All Values by a Constant

content.text: When every value in a dataset is multiplied by the same constant, the mean is also multiplied by that constant. If the original mean is 'a' and we multiply all values by constant 'm', the new mean becomes 'a × m'.
For example, if data {8, 3, 10, 13, 4, 6, 7, 7, 8, 8, 5} has mean 7.18, and we multiply each value by 2, the new data becomes {16, 6, 20, 26, 8, 12, 14, 14, 16, 16, 10} with new mean 14.36.

content.title: Effect of Multiplying by Constants
content.text: If original mean = a, and we multiply all values by constant m, then new mean = a × m. This property is fundamental in data scaling operations used in statistics and data science for standardization and normalization.

content.title: Algebraic Proof: Multiplying by Constant m
content.content: Original mean: a = (X₁ + X₂ + ... + Xₙ) / n. New mean with ×m: = [(m·X₁) + (m·X₂) + ... + (m·Xₙ)] / n = m[(X₁ + X₂ + ... + Xₙ)] / n = m × [(X₁ + X₂ + ... + Xₙ) / n] = m × a. Therefore, new mean = m × a.

topics.topic_name: Fair-Share Interpretation of Transformations

content.text: The mean can be understood through the 'fair-share' perspective. If we redistribute values equally among all individuals, each person gets the mean amount. Transformations (adding, subtracting, multiplying) change what 'fair-share' means in context.

For instance, if average marks = 75 and we add 5 bonus points to everyone, the new fair-share (mean marks) = 80. This makes intuitive sense: if every student's score increased by 5, the average must increase by 5.

topics.topic_name: The Median: Definition and Basic Properties

content.text: The median is the middle value in a sorted dataset. For an odd number of values, it is the exact middle value. For an even number of values, it is typically the average of the two middle values. The key property of the median is that it divides the dataset into two equal halves.

content.title: Median Definition
content.text: The median is the value such that there are an equal number of data points less than it and greater than it. It is position-dependent rather than value-dependent, unlike the mean which considers all values.
Because the median depends only on position, it is often more resistant to outliers and extreme values compared to the mean.

topics.topic_name: Effect of Adding Values on the Median

content.text: When we add a new value to a dataset, the median may or may not change, depending on where the new value falls relative to the current median. If the new value is greater than the current median, it shifts the middle position, potentially increasing the median. If the new value is less than the median, the median may decrease.
Example: Consider dataset {2, 4, 6, 8, 10} with median = 6. If we add 11, the new sorted data is {2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 11}. The new median = (6 + 8) / 2 = 7, which increased from 6.

content.title: Median Changes with New Values
content.text: Adding a value greater than median → median may increase or stay same. Adding a value less than median → median may decrease or stay same. Adding a value equal to median → median stays same (in most cases for even-sized datasets).

content.title: Example: Adding Value Greater Than Median
content.content: Original data: {2, 4, 6, 8} with median = (4 + 6) / 2 = 5. Add value 10. New data: {2, 4, 6, 8, 10}. New median = 6. The median increased because the new value 10 > 5.

topics.topic_name: Comparing Mean and Median Behavior

content.text: While both mean and median measure central tendency, they behave differently when data changes. The mean considers all values, making it sensitive to every data point. The median only depends on position, making it more stable to changes that don't affect the middle position.
In datasets with extreme values (outliers), the median often provides a better representation of the 'typical' value than the mean, which gets pulled toward the extremes.

notes.section_title: 5.1 (Continued) - Finding the Unknown and Mean with Frequencies

topics.topic_name: Finding Missing Values Using the Mean

content.text: Given a dataset with one unknown value and the known mean, we can calculate the missing value using the mean formula. If we know the mean, the number of values, and all but one value, we can use algebraic manipulation to find the unknown.

content.title: Method: Finding Unknown from Mean
content.text: Mean = (Sum of all values) / (Number of values). Rearranging: Unknown value = (Mean × Number of values) - (Sum of known values). This relationship allows us to find the missing data point.

content.title: Example: Missing Weight Problem
content.content: 10 wrestlers with mean weight 39.2 kg. Sum of 9 known weights = 349 kg. Unknown weight w = (39.2 × 10) - 349 = 392 - 349 = 43 kg. Verification: (349 + 43) / 10 = 392 / 10 = 39.2 ✓

content.text: This technique is particularly useful in real-world scenarios like finding a missing measurement when most data is available but one data point is lost or unclear.

topics.topic_name: Mean with Frequency Distribution

content.text: When data contains repeated values (frequencies), we must account for how many times each value appears. The mean is calculated by multiplying each value by its frequency, summing these products, and dividing by the total frequency.

content.title: Mean with Frequencies Formula
content.text: Mean = [Σ(value × frequency)] / [Σ(frequency)]. This accounts for the fact that a value appearing 5 times contributes more to the total than a value appearing once.
For example, if family sizes {3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10} appear with frequencies {3, 11, 9, 7, 3, 1, 1, 1} respectively, we must multiply each size by its frequency before summing.

content.title: Example: Family Size Calculation with Frequencies

topics.topic_name: Median with Frequency Distribution

content.text: To find the median from a frequency distribution, we first determine which position the median falls in by using cumulative frequencies. We add frequencies progressively until we reach or exceed the middle position.

content.title: Method: Finding Median from Frequency Table
content.text: 1) Calculate total frequency (n). 2) Find position of median: (n+1)/2 for odd n, or between n/2 and (n/2)+1 for even n. 3) Use cumulative frequencies to find which value occupies this position.

content.title: Example: Median from Family Size Distribution
content.content: Total frequency = 36 (even). Median position = between 18th and 19th values. Cumulative: 3, 14, 23, 30, ... The 18th and 19th positions fall in the value 5 (since cumulative reaches 23 by value 5). Therefore, median = 5.

content.text: This method avoids writing out all individual values explicitly and is efficient for large datasets presented in frequency table format.

topics.topic_name: Introduction to Spreadsheets for Data Analysis

content.text: A spreadsheet is a digital tool organized in rows and columns containing cells where we can input data, text, or formulas. Spreadsheets automate calculations and make handling large datasets efficient. Each cell is referenced by column letter (A, B, C...) and row number (1, 2, 3...).

content.title: Spreadsheet Basics
content.text: Cells are the basic units where data goes. A range like B3:G3 refers to cells from B3 to G3 in row 3. Formulas like =SUM() and =AVERAGE() perform calculations automatically on selected ranges.
Spreadsheets eliminate manual calculation errors and allow for rapid recalculation if data changes. They are essential tools in modern data analysis across education, business, and research.

topics.topic_name: SUM and AVERAGE Functions

content.text: The SUM function adds all values in a specified range. The AVERAGE function calculates the mean of all values in a range. These functions dramatically speed up calculations compared to manual computation.

content.title: Spreadsheet Formula Syntax
content.text: =SUM(B3:G3) adds all values from B3 to G3. =AVERAGE(B7:D7) calculates the mean of values in B7, C7, D7. These formulas can be copied to other cells, automatically adjusting cell references.

content.title: Example: Computing Total and Average Marks
content.content: To find total marks for a student across 6 subjects in row 3: =SUM(B3:G3). To find class average in subject D (English): =AVERAGE(D2:D23) for 22 students. Formulas automatically update if any value changes.

content.text: Spreadsheets make it possible to analyze large datasets (like marks of hundreds of students across multiple subjects) in seconds, which would take hours or days to compute manually.

topics.topic_name: Practical Applications in Data Organization

content.text: Spreadsheets are used to organize data systematically, perform calculations, identify patterns, and create visual representations. In educational contexts, teachers use them to record grades, calculate averages, and track student performance.
In business and research, spreadsheets handle inventory, financial data, experimental results, and market trends. They bridge the gap between raw data collection and meaningful analysis.

topics.topic_name: Introduction to Data Visualization

content.text: Data visualization converts numerical information into graphical representations, making patterns and trends visible at a glance. Visual representations help communicate data insights more effectively than tables of numbers.
Different types of data require different visualization methods. Understanding when to use bar graphs, line graphs, pie charts, or other representations is crucial for effective communication.

topics.topic_name: Understanding Line Graphs

content.text: Line graphs consist of points connected by line segments, typically with the x-axis (horizontal) representing one variable and the y-axis (vertical) representing another. Line graphs are particularly effective for showing how values change over time.

content.title: When to Use Line Graphs
content.text: Line graphs are best for: continuous data, time-series data, showing trends over time, comparing multiple series simultaneously. They show change direction and rate of change through the slope of line segments.

The steepness of a line segment indicates the rate of change. A steep segment shows rapid change; a flat segment shows little or no change. Comparing slopes allows us to compare rates of change between different series or time periods.

content.title: Example: Temperature Trends
content.content: A line graph showing monthly maximum temperatures for two states. Punjab's line shows steeper slopes from January-June (rapid temperature increase) then flattens July-September (minimal change), then steep decline toward December (rapid cooling). Kerala's line stays relatively flat year-round (stable temperature). The visualization makes these patterns immediately clear.

topics.topic_name: Two-Step Process for Reading Line Graphs

content.text: Reading a line graph effectively involves two steps: (1) Identify what is given by examining the graph's organization, scales, and patterns, and (2) Infer and interpret from what is shown by analyzing trends and drawing conclusions.

content.title: Step 1: Identify What is Given
content.text: Notice: axis labels and units, scale values, data points and how they're marked, line colors and styles if multiple series are shown, any legends or keys provided. Understanding the 'what' before drawing conclusions prevents misinterpretation.

content.title: Step 2: Infer and Interpret
content.text: Analyze patterns: upward/downward trends, peaks and valleys, rate of change, comparisons between series. Draw conclusions: which values are highest/lowest, when do changes occur, what might explain the patterns. Consider limitations of the data shown.

content.title: Example: Temperature Graph Analysis
content.content: Identify: X-axis shows months (Jan-Dec), Y-axis shows temperature (°C). Two colored lines for Kerala and Punjab. Infer: Punjab peaks at 38°C in June and drops to 19°C in January (high variation). Kerala stays between 29-33°C year-round (low variation). Conclusion: Punjab experiences seasonal temperature extremes; Kerala has a consistent warm climate.

topics.topic_name: Advantages of Line Graphs Over Other Representations

content.text: Line graphs are more efficient than bar graphs or column graphs for displaying time-series data with many data points. A clustered-column graph showing 13 years of data for 4 entities would require 52 bars, appearing crowded and difficult to interpret. A line graph with 4 lines shows the same data clearly and compactly.

The connected line segments in a line graph naturally show continuity and flow of data over time. This makes it easier to identify trends, turning points, and acceleration/deceleration in change compared to disconnected bar segments.

content.title: Why Line Graphs Excel for Time-Series
content.text: Connected points emphasize continuity and trends. Slopes show rate of change visually. Multiple series can be compared easily. Patterns become immediately obvious. Less cluttered than bar graphs for many data points. Most effective for showing change over time.

topics.topic_name: Applications: Space Objects and Rainfall Data

content.text: Line graphs effectively visualize global space object launches (showing increase from ~1000 annually in 2012 to ~2800 in 2024) and monthly rainfall patterns (showing seasonal monsoon variations across different cities). These real-world examples demonstrate the versatility of line graphs.

For space data, line slopes show acceleration in launch rates. For rainfall, the graph reveals that west coast cities (Kovalam, Udupi, Mumbai) peak during June-August (southwest monsoon), while east coast cities (Chennai, Rameswaram) peak during October-December (northeast monsoon).

content.title: Example: Rainfall Pattern Analysis
content.content: Line graph of monthly rainfall for 6 Indian cities shows clear geographical patterns. West coast cities have sharp peaks June-August reaching 24-28 days of rain. East coast cities peak later (Oct-Dec). This visualization makes monsoon patterns immediately obvious without needing to examine numerical tables.

topics.topic_name: Understanding Data Collection Methods

content.text: When interpreting visualized data, it's important to understand how the data was collected. This knowledge helps identify potential biases, limitations, and the reliability of conclusions we can draw. Data collected through different methods may have different levels of accuracy and completeness.

For example, monthly average rainfall is calculated by collecting daily rainfall data over multiple years for each city, then averaging the totals for each month. This means a single data point represents an aggregation and average, not a single year's measurement.

content.title: Data Collection Awareness
content.text: Understanding collection methods helps identify: potential sources of error, whether data is aggregated or individual, time periods represented, geographical or population coverage, and reliability of conclusions. Misunderstanding collection methods leads to incorrect interpretation.

topics.topic_name: Drawing Conclusions Carefully

content.text: Valid inferences from data must be grounded in what the graph actually shows, not in what we assume or expect. A statement like 'Nepal did not launch any space objects from 2012-2024' is valid only if the graph specifically shows Nepal with zero data points or explicitly states this. If Nepal simply isn't shown, we cannot conclude this.

Similarly, if a line graph shows data for only certain countries, we cannot infer values for countries not included. The graph only shows what is explicitly presented; absence from the graph doesn't confirm absence of activity.

content.title: Valid vs Invalid Inferences
content.text: Valid: conclusions directly supported by the data shown. Invalid: conclusions requiring information not in the graph, assumptions about missing entities, overgeneralizations beyond the data's scope, or mixing of correlation with causation.

content.title: Example: Valid and Invalid Statements
content.content: Valid: 'In 2023, USA launched more objects than China or Russia individually' (if the graph shows this). Invalid: 'India did not launch space objects in this period' (if India is not shown on the graph). Invalid: 'Higher launches cause technological advancement' (correlation ≠ causation).

topics.topic_name: Activity Strips as Data Representation

content.text: An activity strip is a timeline representation where a day is divided into equal time intervals (e.g., 30-minute blocks) and each interval is colored according to the activity performed. A full day strip has 48 boxes (24 hours ÷ 30 minutes), providing a detailed visual representation of time allocation.

content.title: Reading Activity Strips
content.text: Each color represents a different activity (sleeping, eating, classes, hobbies, exercise, etc.). The length of colored sections shows time spent on each activity. Comparing strips for different days reveals patterns in daily routines and differences between weekdays and weekends.

Activity strips provide a holistic view of how time is distributed throughout a day, making patterns and imbalances visually apparent. They are useful for time management analysis and understanding daily routines.

content.title: Example: Analyzing Activity Strips
content.content: If a Friday strip shows long continuous colored sections for 'hobbies/entertainment' during evening hours, while a school day strip shows 'classes/study' occupying mid-day hours, this reveals how time allocation differs between school days and leisure days.

topics.topic_name: Comparing Patterns Across Multiple Time Periods

content.text: By creating activity strips for different days (weekdays vs weekends, school days vs vacation days, school weeks vs holiday weeks), we can visually compare and analyze how routines change. These comparisons reveal consistent patterns and highlight how external factors affect time allocation.
Comparing strips across family members (students vs adults) shows how different individuals allocate time differently based on their responsibilities and lifestyle.

topics.topic_name: Sleep Patterns Across Age Groups

content.text: Line graphs representing sleep duration across ages (e.g., 6-75 years) reveal important patterns: young children sleep ~9.5 hours daily, this decreases through teenage years, stabilizes at ~8 hours in adulthood (ages 30-50), then increases slightly to ~8.5 hours after age 50.

These patterns reflect biological changes and lifestyle variations across ages. Children need more sleep for growth and development. Elderly individuals may experience changes in sleep patterns due to health conditions or lifestyle changes.

content.title: Smooth Line Graphs
content.text: When a line graph contains many data points closely spaced, it appears as a smooth curve rather than distinct line segments. This occurs with 80+ data points in the sleep age study. Smooth curves are more visually effective than many individual bars or segments.

topics.topic_name: Interpreting Complex Multi-Series Data

content.text: When graphs show multiple series (e.g., births by month across multiple years, rainfall patterns for multiple cities), interpretation requires careful reading to avoid false conclusions. Missing or incomplete data, scale changes, or overlapping lines can create confusion.

Infographics—graphical representations using maps, colors, and visual elements beyond traditional axes—offer another approach to displaying complex geographic or categorical data, such as regional preferences for rice vs. wheat consumption.

glossary.definition: The sum of all values divided by the number of values. Represents the balance point of a dataset where the sum of distances to values below equals the sum of distances to values above.
The middle value when data is arranged in sorted order. For odd-sized datasets, it is the exact middle value. For even-sized datasets, it is the average of the two middle values.
A statistical measure that describes the center or typical value of a dataset. The mean, median, and mode are all measures of central tendency.
A value in a dataset where the sum of distances to all values below it equals the sum of distances to all values above it. The mean is the unique balance point for any dataset.
A table or graph showing the values in a dataset and how often each value appears. Used to efficiently represent data where values are repeated.
The running total of frequencies as we move from the smallest to the largest value in a frequency distribution. Used to find the median and other position-based statistics.
A digital tool organized in rows and columns containing cells where data, text, or formulas can be entered. Used for organizing, calculating, and analyzing data.
A graph with points connected by line segments, typically used to show how values change over time. Effective for displaying trends, patterns, and rate of change.
The graphical representation of numerical data. Includes bar graphs, line graphs, pie charts, histograms, and other visual forms that make patterns and relationships visible.
A general direction or pattern in data over time. Can be upward (increasing), downward (decreasing), or flat (stable).
An extreme data value that differs significantly from other values in the dataset. Outliers can disproportionately affect the mean but have less effect on the median.
The speed at which a value changes over time. In a line graph, it is represented by the slope of the line segment—steeper slopes indicate faster change.
A visual representation that combines graphs, maps, and other graphical elements to present complex information clearly and appealingly.

A timeline representation of daily activities where a 24-hour day is divided into equal time intervals, each colored to represent a different activity. Useful for analyzing time allocation and routines.
A conclusion drawn based on evidence presented in data or graphs. Valid inferences are supported by the actual data; invalid inferences go beyond what the data shows.

summary_points: The mean is the balance point of a dataset where the sum of distances from the mean to values below it equals the sum of distances to values above it.
The mean is unique—there is exactly one value for any dataset that satisfies the balance property.
Adding a value greater than the mean increases the mean; adding a value less than the mean decreases the mean; adding a value equal to the mean keeps it unchanged.

When a constant is added to all values in a dataset, the mean increases by that constant. When a constant is subtracted from all values, the mean decreases by that constant. When all values are multiplied by a constant, the mean is multiplied by that same constant.
The median is the middle value in a sorted dataset that divides the data into two equal halves. Unlike the mean, the median is position-dependent rather than value-dependent.
Adding values greater than the median may increase it; adding values less than the median may decrease it.
When one value is missing from a dataset with a known mean, we can find it using: Unknown = (Mean × Count of values) - (Sum of known values).
For frequency distributions, the mean is calculated as: Mean = Σ(value × frequency) / Σ(frequency), accounting for how many times each value appears.
The median from a frequency distribution is found using cumulative frequencies to determine which value occupies the middle position.
Spreadsheets use formulas like =SUM() and =AVERAGE() to automate calculations on large datasets, eliminating manual computation errors and allowing rapid recalculation.
Line graphs are most effective for displaying time-series data and continuous change. They show trends, rates of change, and patterns more clearly than bar graphs for many data points.

Reading a line graph effectively involves two steps: (1) Identify what is given by examining the graph structure, scales, and data representation, and (2) Infer and interpret by analyzing trends and drawing conclusions based only on what is shown.
Understanding how data is collected and what it represents is crucial for valid interpretation. Missing data or absent entities cannot be confirmed as non-existent.
Valid inferences from graphs must be supported by the data shown; invalid inferences go beyond the data's scope or make unjustified assumptions.
Activity strips represent how time is allocated throughout a day, with each color representing a different activity. Comparing strips reveals patterns and differences in daily routines.
